content: Implementing gcd in safe Rust is... possibly easier than safely wrapping gcd() [which takes unsigned longs instead of sized-types :confused:]. How should r4l code calculate gcd's? drm/asahi wants this
What is gcd :grimacing: ?
I think wrapping the existing function is fine. It would take usizes from a Rust perspective.

Alice Ryhl said:

I think wrapping the existing function is fine. It would take usizes from a Rust perspective.

Right. Is that... what users actually want? In my use case we want u64 gcd. yes, we're on aarch64 but all the extra casting in the driver to use the usize version seems sketchy. IDK

My uninformed opinion: Upsizing everything to usize could have some bad implications if you're doing gcd on a 64-bit integer on a 32-bit architecture. As someone who writes Rust regularly, it always feels kind of, idk, "gross".
On one hand, it's probably less of maintenance pain to wrap gdc() like everything else. On the other, there'll have to be a rust/math.rs file anyways so we could just re-code gcd() in it since it saves time crossing the FFI barrier for things that are trivial to implement Rust-side. The only optimized bit in the C part is if CONFIG_CPU_NO_EFFICIENT_FFS is disabled, and it uses the binary algorithm. Otherwise, it's the even/odd algorithm.

(actually i'm not even sure there's significant time loss from the FFI but i can go check)

Amélie Gonzalez said:

Upsizing everything to usize could have some bad implications if you're doing gcd on a 64-bit integer on a 32-bit architecture

The problem in the other direction is even spicier - it means we can't do 64-bit gcd at all on 32-bit architecutres, which is surprising behaviour at the least.

Alyssa Rosenzweig said:

Amélie Gonzalez said:

Upsizing everything to usize could have some bad implications if you're doing gcd on a 64-bit integer on a 32-bit architecture

The problem in the other direction is even spicier - it means we can't do 64-bit gcd at all on 32-bit architecutres, which is surprising behaviour at the least.

Oh yeah that seems to already be a problem with the C version. It's odd that nobody complained. Perhaps nobody had to do the GCD of two 64-bit numbers on a 32-bit arch, or they found ways around it.
In native Rust i guess we could have a constraint for two integers that can bit-and and bit-or each other and that we can Copy, that should cover all integers (and booleans if someone fancies that). Different sizes would probably optimize their algos differently though, i assume.

While i'm at it, i don't think anybody ever had to write this before, so if anyone needs it at some point, it's free code:

/// Swap two mutable variables that are `Copy`
macro_rules! swap {
    ($a:ident, $b:ident) => {
        let _tmp = $a;
        $a = $b;
        $b = _tmp;
    };
}

For small numbers (1014, 624):

[   26.148922] rust_gcd: Rust benchmark for 10M GCD of two usize:
[   26.149249] rust_gcd: NATIVE: 193ms, BINDING: 281ms

For two bigger numbers (32879183 and 37929911):

[   33.521935] rust_gcd: Rust benchmark for 10M GCD of two usize:
[   33.522073] rust_gcd: NATIVE: 1298ms, BINDING: 1189ms

Both functions are #[inline]ed in Rust. i guess the cost of FFI amortizes with how optimized the C algo is VS using things like usize::overflowing_neg and usize::trailing_zeros?
while you're at it, could you try https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250319205141.3528424-1-gary@garyguo.net/ ?
note you will add __rust_helper before binding functions to make it work.

Boqun Feng said:

note you will add __rust_helper before binding functions to make it work.

s/will add/will need to add
We can have a 32-bit version of the helper that calls the usize version if the numbers fit, otherwise it runs the gcd algorithm in Rust until they do.

Boqun Feng said:

while you're at it, could you try https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250319205141.3528424-1-gary@garyguo.net/ ?

Just so i understood correctly: i should re-bench but on top of your patch so that i can fully inline the binding to gcd()?
For context, gcd is not inline, bindgen picks it up perfectly; there's no real need to write a helper for it

[   21.962798] rust_gcd: Rust benchmark for 10M GCD of two usize (gcd(32879183,37929911)=1):
[   21.963332] rust_gcd: NATIVE: 1319824698ns, INLINE HELPER: 68310ns
[   22.182880] rust_gcd: Rust benchmark for 10M GCD of two usize (gcd(624,1014)=78):
[   22.182994] rust_gcd: NATIVE: 219272547ns, INLINE HELPER: 36569ns

With @Boqun Feng 's inline helper patch (from Gary, if i understood correctly), after making gcd always inline so that bindgen no longer picks it up. Either i messed up something really badly or this is good. Having a look at the disassembly of my module seems to confirm that the C helper was indeed correctly inlined.
Thanks for trying this out, the number looks promising
